stories.headline: US strikes Iran's Qeshm Island as Iranian missiles and drones hit Kuwait airport and target Bahrain
stories.summary: US Central Command struck an Iranian military ground-control station on Qeshm Island in the Strait of Hormuz on June 3 after Iran launched ballistic missiles and drones at Gulf targets. Missiles aimed at Kuwait fell short and three fired at Bahrain were intercepted, but a drone hit Kuwait's main airport, killing at least one person (an Indian national) and injuring dozens. The exchange followed reports that ceasefire-extension talks had stalled.

perspectives.keyFraming: Tit-for-tat strikes mark a sharp escalation as ceasefire diplomacy appears to falter.
perspectives.summary: Iran's Revolutionary Guards said they targeted the US Fifth Fleet headquarters in Bahrain and other US facilities; the US said it intercepted most missiles and struck Qeshm Island in response. A drone hitting Kuwait's main airport caused civilian casualties as reports indicated that talks to extend a ceasefire had broken down.

sources.title: Iran fires missiles and US strikes Iran facility after reports of faltering peace talks

perspectives.keyFraming: Civilian toll mounts as an Iranian drone hits Kuwait's main airport, killing a foreign worker.
perspectives.summary: Al Jazeera reported a drone struck Kuwait's main airport, killing an Indian citizen and injuring more than 60 people, while Tehran said the US had attacked Qeshm Island. Coverage centered the civilian impact and the risk to Gulf aviation and shipping as both sides claimed strikes on the other.

sources.title: Iranian drone attack kills Indian citizen in Kuwait after US strikes Qeshm

perspectives.keyFraming: US forces shoot down Iranian missiles and drones in defense of Gulf allies.
perspectives.summary: Fox News emphasized US and Bahraini air defenses intercepting Iranian missiles aimed at Bahrain and the US military response on Qeshm, framing the strikes as protecting American forces and Gulf partners against Iranian aggression after diplomacy stalled.

sources.title: US forces shoot down Iranian missiles, drones in response to fresh attacks on Kuwait, Bahrain

stories.headline: Senate advances Iran War Powers resolution for the first time after GOP Sen. Cassidy flips
stories.summary: The Senate voted 50-47 on June 2 to advance a resolution directing President Trump to end hostilities with Iran — the first time such a motion cleared a procedural hurdle after several failed attempts. Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-La.), who had just lost his renomination bid, flipped to 'yes,' joining Collins, Murkowski and Paul. The House passed a companion resolution 215-208 the next day; both still face a likely Trump veto.

perspectives.keyFraming: A bipartisan congressional rebuke is finally moving to constrain a war critics say Trump waged without authorization.
perspectives.summary: Left-leaning coverage emphasized the historic nature of both chambers moving to rein in Trump, framing it as a democratic check on a conflict the public broadly opposes. It highlighted Republicans breaking ranks and complaints that the administration kept Congress in the dark on the operation.

sources.title: House passes war powers resolution directing Trump to end hostilities with Iran
House adopts war powers resolution to rein in Trump on Iran

perspectives.keyFraming: After several attempts, a single Republican defection tipped the Senate into uncharted territory on checking the president’s war powers.
perspectives.summary: Center outlets stressed the mechanics: the 50-47 vote, Cassidy's flip days after his primary loss, and that absent Republicans could still affect final passage. They noted any resolution would face a near-certain Trump veto requiring two-thirds majorities in both chambers to override.

sources.title: Senate advances resolution to limit Trump’s Iran war powers for first time
Senate advances bill aimed at ending Iran war as Cassidy, after primary loss, flips to support it

perspectives.keyFraming: Critics on the right say the measure handcuffs the commander-in-chief mid-conflict, with a lame-duck senator providing the assist.
perspectives.summary: Right-leaning coverage framed the measure as weakening Trump's negotiating leverage with Tehran and cast Cassidy's flip as sour grapes after his primary defeat, while emphasizing the resolution's likely veto and longstanding questions about the War Powers Act's constitutionality.

sources.title: Senate Democrats advance resolution to limit Trump’s Iran war powers
House passes war powers resolution to end hostilities in Iran

stories.headline: US-Iran strikes near the Strait of Hormuz push oil toward $100 and pull stocks off record highs
stories.summary: A fragile US-Iran ceasefire frayed further as both sides traded missile and drone attacks. Iranian strikes hit Kuwait — killing at least one and shutting its main airport — while US Central Command said it conducted defensive strikes near the Strait of Hormuz. Brent traded near $100 and WTI in the mid-$90s as US equities retreated from records and Treasury yields rose.

perspectives.keyFraming: Renewed strikes after a collapsed ceasefire reignite supply-disruption fears, pricing a geopolitical risk premium into oil and bonds.
perspectives.summary: Markets coverage framed June 3-4 as a dangerous escalation: Iranian attacks on Kuwait and US strikes near Hormuz revived fears over the roughly fifth of global oil and LNG transiting the strait. Oil rose toward $100, Treasuries sold off with the 10-year near 4.49%, and the S&P 500 and Dow retreated from records as investors weighed how long the conflict could drag on.

sources.title: Stocks slip and oil jumps on fresh US-Iran clashes: Markets Wrap

perspectives.keyFraming: Regional outlets emphasize the conflict spreading to Gulf neighbors and stalled diplomacy as Iran threatens to fully close Hormuz.
perspectives.summary: Al Arabiya and RFE/RL emphasized the conflict spreading to Gulf neighbors — Iranian attacks on Kuwait and US defensive strikes in southern Iran and on Qeshm Island — and stalled diplomacy as Iran halted talks and threatened to fully close the Strait of Hormuz, keeping a risk premium on crude.

sources.title: Iran attacks Kuwait, US strikes near Hormuz as diplomacy stalls
Iran, US exchange attacks as tensions in Gulf rise

stories.headline: China’s DeepSeek nears $7.4B first funding round at up to $59B valuation, backed by Tencent and CATL
stories.summary: DeepSeek is close to finalizing roughly 50 billion yuan ($7.4 billion) in its first external funding round, valuing the open-source AI firm between $52 billion and $59 billion. Tencent and battery maker CATL are the largest backers, alongside a state-backed national AI fund. Founder Liang Wenfeng is expected to contribute about 40%. It would rank among China's largest tech fundraisings.

perspectives.keyFraming: DeepSeek’s first capital raise signals state-backed scaling of China’s open-source AI champion.
perspectives.summary: Bloomberg and CNBC reported DeepSeek nearing roughly $7.4 billion at a $52-59 billion valuation, with Tencent, CATL and a state AI fund signing term sheets. The maker of the V3 and R1 models previously scaled without outside venture capital; the round, with heavy founder participation, marks a strategic shift and a notable Beijing endorsement.

sources.title: DeepSeek close to sealing $7 billion funding in historic AI deal
DeepSeek slated to draw $7 billion in maiden fundraising, sources say

perspectives.keyFraming: Asian outlets frame the round as validation of China’s AI self-reliance drive amid tightening US export controls.
perspectives.summary: The South China Morning Post framed the raise as proof of China's push for AI self-sufficiency, with Tencent, CATL and state funds backing DeepSeek as Washington tightens chip-export controls on Chinese firms.

sources.title: China’s DeepSeek nears US$7 billion haul in first-ever funding round

stories.headline: Trump signs order reclassifying about 8,000 senior federal workers as at-will employees
stories.summary: President Trump signed an executive order on June 3 moving roughly 8,000 senior policy-influencing positions into a new 'Schedule Policy/Career' category, letting agencies remove them without standard procedural protections. The White House says it boosts accountability; federal unions called it a politicization of the civil service and pointed to pending lawsuits.

perspectives.keyFraming: Critics say Trump is stripping civil-service protections to enable political purges of career experts.
perspectives.summary: Left coverage led with the loss of due-process and appeal rights and union warnings that the order lets the administration fire workers for political reasons, situating it within the broader Schedule F revival and ongoing litigation alleging statutory violations.

sources.title: Trump strips job protections from 8,000 federal workers
Trump makes it easier to fire 8,000 federal workers by making them at-will employees

perspectives.keyFraming: The order formalizes a long-telegraphed reclassification affecting mostly senior policy staff while leaving some protections intact.
perspectives.summary: Trade and center outlets detailed the scope — about 97% at or above GS-15 — and noted retained whistleblower protections and a ban on political-affiliation firings, but loss of appeal rights. The count is far below an earlier 50,000 estimate and the policy faces multiple union lawsuits.

sources.title: Trump signs order moving thousands of federal employees into Schedule F
Trump formalizes move of career federal workers into ‘at will’ roles

perspectives.keyFraming: Supporters frame the order as delivering long-promised accountability, making career bureaucrats answerable to elected leadership.
perspectives.summary: Right-leaning coverage framed the order as restoring accountability and ensuring career staff carry out the president's agenda, citing the White House rationale that removals target poor performance, misconduct or subversion of presidential directives.

sources.title: Unions condemn Trump order making federal workers ‘at-will’ employees
Fact Sheet: President Trump Increases Accountability in the Federal Workforce
